text: The robotic near total gastrectomy begins with the surgeon entering through the gastrocolic omentum using a five trocar technique. The dissection proceeds to the patient's right, further opening the gastrocolic omentum, and continues along the short gastric vessels to separate the stomach from the spleen.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the short gastric vessels, staying close to the spleen, using bipolar cautery. The stomach is freed from the ventral surface of the pancreas, and the dissection continues along the edge of the colon to the patient's right, moving cephalad until reaching the duodenum. The dissection proceeds along the undersurface of the liver to mobilize the duodenum, and the gastropathic omentum is opened. The dissection is carried to the right crus and along the ventral surface of the esophagus.

The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic omentum widely in a stellate fashion, facilitating dissection across the gastroplog vessels. The duodenum and pylorus are identified, ensuring no antrum remains on the duodenal stump. The duodenum is divided, and the stump is folded onto the ventral surface of the pancreas. The proximal stomach is divided, and the left gastric artery with its nodal arcade is taken with the specimen, leaving a tube of stomach along the greater curve.

The surgeon staples the stomach and uses bipolar cautery to divide the remaining tissues. The excised specimen is placed into an extraction bag and removed through the assistance port in the patient's left lower quadrant. The stomach is then brought into proximity with the jejunum using a barbed suture, creating a running suture line.

The robotic system offers enhanced visualization and ergonomic support for suturing. The surgeon completes the back row of the anastomosis, leaving sutures with engaged needles for the subsequent ventral row. The jejunum is opened using monopolar scissors.

The surgeon uses a barbed monofilament suture to create the inner layer of the anastomosis, placing sutures in the middle of the back row and running them to each corner. The robotic system facilitates this one-person operation, allowing precise suturing without much need for assistance.

The surgeon completes the inner layer of the anastomosis by sewing the suture from the middle of the back row to each corner, tying it off, and then proceeding to the ventral portion. The second layer of the anastomosis is completed using the suture from the back wall. An endoscopy is performed, showing no air bubbles over the stomach when saline is applied, indicating a successful anastomosis. The surgical wounds are closed with Maxon, followed by Vicryl and Steristrips.
